source_paper_title: Monotone Causality in Opportunistically Stochastic Shortest Path Problems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Gaspard_2025, title={Monotone Causality in Opportunistically Stochastic Shortest Path Problems}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0362}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0362}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Gaspard, Mallory E. and Vladimirsky, Alexander}, year={2025}, month=jun }
context:
Section 6 (Conclusions), page 33 (near the end)

Another interesting direction for future work is to find criteria for applicability of label-setting to “stochastic shortest path games,” in which the probability distribution over the successor nodes at each stage depends on the actions chosen by two antagonistic players [46]. We note that some results on the applicability of Dijkstra’s method to deterministic games on graphs have been developed in the last ten years [7, 11], but the case of general stochastic games on graphs remains open.
title: Criteria for label-setting applicability in stochastic shortest path games on graphs
background_latex:
A stochastic shortest path game is a two-player, zero-sum sequential decision problem on a finite state space (graph) \(X=\{x_1,\dots,x_n,t\}\) with an absorbing target state \(t\). At each nonterminal state \(x\in X\setminus\{t\}\), two antagonistic players (e.g., Player 1 minimizes total cost and Player 2 maximizes it) choose actions \(a\in A_1(x)\) and \(b\in A_2(x)\). The chosen pair \((a,b)\) incurs an immediate cost \(C(x,a,b)\) and induces a transition probability distribution \(p(x,a,b,\cdot)\) over successor states in \(X\). A (possibly history-dependent) strategy profile induces a stochastic process \((y_k)_{k\ge 0}\) with \(y_0=x\) and the game terminates upon first reaching \(t\), after which no further cost is incurred.

The objective is typically the expected total accumulated cost until absorption at \(t\), yielding a value function \(U(x)\) (the game value) that satisfies a dynamic programming / Shapley optimality equation of the form
\[
U(x)=\min_{a\in A_1(x)}\max_{b\in A_2(x)}\Bigl\{C(x,a,b)+\sum_{y\in X} p(x,a,b,y)\,U(y)\Bigr\},\qquad U(t)=0,
\]
(or with \(\max\min\) depending on modeling and assumptions ensuring a value).

A label-setting method (Dijkstra-like) for shortest paths computes \(U\) by progressively finalizing (“accepting”) states in nondecreasing order of their true values, relying on a monotonic causality property: whenever \(U(x)\) depends on \(U(y)\) through an optimal action choice, one must have \(U(x)\ge U(y)\) (or a strict/\(\delta\)-separated variant) so that accepted labels never need revision.
problem_definition_latex: Determine verifiable (ideally a priori and local) sufficient conditions under which a label-setting algorithm (e.g., a Dijkstra-like method, and possibly a \(\delta\)-bucket variant akin to Dial’s method) correctly computes the value function \(U\) of a stochastic shortest path game on a graph, i.e., conditions guaranteeing a monotonic causality property that makes label-setting applicable.

Determine verifiable (ideally a priori and local) sufficient conditions under which a label-setting algorithm (e.g., a Dijkstra-like method, and possibly a \(\delta\)-bucket variant akin to Dial’s method) correctly computes the value function \(U\) of a stochastic shortest path game on a graph, i.e., conditions guaranteeing a monotonic causality property that makes label-setting applicable.
open_problem_quote: Another interesting direction for future work is to find criteria for applicability of label-setting to “stochastic shortest path games,” in which the probability distribution over the successor nodes at each stage depends on the actions chosen by two antagonistic players [46]. We note that some results on the applicability of Dijkstra’s method to deterministic games on graphs have been developed in the last ten years [7, 11], but the case of general stochastic games on graphs remains open.
subject_classification: stochastic shortest path games and dynamic programming
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can generate small finite stochastic games and test whether a proposed local condition fails by comparing a label-setting output against the exact Shapley-equation solution (e.g., via value iteration or linear programming for discounted/absorbing variants). However, the open question asks for general applicability criteria, not a single conjectured condition, so automated search is only moderately helpful without a precise candidate criterion to refute.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: If one fixes a specific algorithmic template (a label-setting procedure) and a candidate sufficient condition, fitness can be evaluated automatically by checking correctness across many randomly generated games. But evolving the right theoretical condition itself is hard to parameterize, and the space of possible conditions is not clearly specified, limiting fully automated progress.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
